coding_scale.1.0: ✓ Fully covered — dimension explicitly defined and operationalized
coding_scale.0.5: ◑ Partially covered — mentioned but not operationally defined, or only implicit
coding_scale.0.0: ✗ Not covered — dimension absent from the framework

frameworks.citation: Hubbard, P. (2011). Evaluation of courseware and websites. In L. Ducate & N. Arnold (Eds.), Present and Future Promises of CALL. CALICO, pp. 407-440.

D1_TECH.evidence: Process-oriented framework focuses on courseware usability and teacher fit but does not cover system architecture, APIs, or technical reliability metrics.

D2_PED.evidence: Comprehensively covers instructional design (method fit, presentation, task naturalness). The 'learner fit' and 'teacher fit' criteria are pedagogically grounded.

D3_INST.evidence: No institutional governance, analytics, or administrative decision-support components.

D4_HighStakes.evidence: Framework is agnostic to test type. No IELTS/TOEFL specificity, CEFR alignment, or washback considerations.

D6_MultiStakeholder.evidence: Teacher-centered evaluation (method fit from teacher perspective). Administrator and institution not represented.

D5_Rigor.evidence: Provides categories and questions for each criterion but without explicit measurement scales.

frameworks.citation: Leakey, J. (2011). Evaluating Computer-Assisted Language Learning: An Integrated Approach. Peter Lang.

D1_TECH.evidence: Includes technical reliability and interface dimensions ('learner support' covers some technical aspects) but not fully systematized as technology architecture.

D2_PED.evidence: 12 criteria including SLA-grounded criteria (interaction, feedback, scaffolding), cognitive load, and skill coverage. Most comprehensive pedagogical coverage in CALL literature.

D3_INST.evidence: No institutional governance or administrative perspective despite its comprehensive approach.

D4_HighStakes.evidence: Designed for general CALL evaluation; no high-stakes test specificity.

D6_MultiStakeholder.evidence: Primarily learner-centered with some teacher perspective, but no administrator or institutional dimension.

D5_Rigor.evidence: 12 criteria with definitions but operationalization remains qualitative.

frameworks.citation: Colpaert, J. (2004). Design of online interactive language courseware. PhD dissertation / conceptual model.

D1_TECH.evidence: RBRO (Requirements, Blueprint, Realization, Operation) focuses on instructional design process, not technical architecture evaluation.

D2_PED.evidence: Covers requirements analysis, learning objectives, task design. Pedagogically comprehensive at the design level.

D3_INST.evidence: Process framework for designers; no institutional governance or governance indicators.

D4_HighStakes.evidence: Generic language learning design; no high-stakes specificity.

D5_Rigor.evidence: Design methodology with phases; not a scoring rubric for evaluation.

frameworks.citation: Rosell-Aguilar, F. (2017). State of the App. CALICO Journal, 34(2), 243-258. DOI: 10.1558/cj.31568 (⚠️ 404 on OpenAlex — verify via CALICO directly)
frameworks.doi_note: 404 on OpenAlex. CALICO Journal not indexed in OpenAlex. Verify via Scopus or CALICO website.

D1_TECH.evidence: Taxonomy includes App Store metadata (device compatibility, size) and interface dimensions but not full technical architecture.

D2_PED.evidence: Covers language skill coverage and pedagogical approach categories but lacks operational definitions for each criterion.

D3_INST.evidence: Mobile app evaluation taxonomy; no institutional governance.

D4_HighStakes.evidence: General MALL taxonomy; no high-stakes test specificity.

D5_Rigor.evidence: Checklist format without scoring rubrics or quantitative criteria.

frameworks.citation: Almaiah, M.A., Al-Khasawneh, A., & Althunibat, A. (2021). Exploring the critical challenges and factors influencing e-learning system usage during COVID-19. Education and Information Technologies, 26(5), 5261-5280.
frameworks.doi_note: ⚠️ Verify via Springer/Scopus. This is the Almaiah 2021 e-learning challenges paper, not always the same as their 6-dimension Delphi framework paper. Confirm the framework is from this source or find correct citation.

D1_TECH.evidence: System quality including technical reliability, internet connectivity, device compatibility explicitly modeled.

D2_PED.evidence: Content quality covered but domain-generic (not language-specific).

D3_INST.evidence: Institutional support and management factors included but not operationalized as analytics or governance instruments.

D6_MultiStakeholder.evidence: Both student and instructor perspectives covered; administrator only partially.

frameworks.citation: Scheffel, M., Drachsler, H., Stoyanov, S., & Specht, M. (2014). Quality indicators for learning analytics. Educational Technology & Society, 17(4), 117-132.
frameworks.doi_note: ⚠️ No stable DOI found. Educational Technology & Society vol.17 no.4. URL: https://www.j-ets.net. Verify Scopus indexing for this specific volume/year.

D1_TECH.evidence: Learning analytics dashboard quality framework; focuses on analytics outputs not system architecture.

D2_PED.evidence: No pedagogical effectiveness dimension; evaluation is of analytics tool performance, not learning content.

D3_INST.evidence: Core framework is institutional/administrative — covers transparency, organizational adoption, accountability, multi-stakeholder reporting.

D4_HighStakes.evidence: Generic LA quality; no testing specificity.

D6_MultiStakeholder.evidence: Includes student and instructor perspectives in quality indicators but administrator remains the primary user.

D5_Rigor.evidence: Quality indicators defined with explanations; not fully quantified.

frameworks.citation: Park, Y. & Jo, I.-H. (2019). Development of the learning analytics dashboard. Journal of Computer Assisted Learning, 35(4), 556-568. DOI: 10.1111/jcal.12354
frameworks.doi_note: ⚠️ OpenAlex returns 'Issue Information' for this DOI — the DOI may point to the issue front matter, not the specific article. Find correct article DOI via Scopus/WoS.

D1_TECH.evidence: Dashboard design framework; does not evaluate underlying platform architecture.

D2_PED.evidence: Analytics-focused; learning content quality not evaluated.

D3_INST.evidence: Comprehensive institutional governance: Kirkpatrick-based evaluation, learning analytics design, decision-support mechanisms.

D6_MultiStakeholder.evidence: Student and instructor views considered in dashboard design; administration partially covered.

D5_Rigor.evidence: Kirkpatrick levels provide some structure but not a quantitative scoring system.

frameworks.citation: Venkatesh, V., Morris, M.G., Davis, G.B., & Davis, F.D. (2003). User acceptance of information technology: Toward a unified view. MIS Quarterly, 27(3), 425-478.
frameworks.doi_note: Standard UTAUT citation. Included conditionally — adopted as evaluation lens by Al-Fraihat et al. (2020) for platform quality.

D1_TECH.evidence: Performance Expectancy and Effort Expectancy partially cover system quality (ease of use, usefulness) but do not operationalize architecture quality.

D2_PED.evidence: No pedagogical dimension; acceptance model only.

D3_INST.evidence: Facilitating Conditions touches on infrastructure support but not governance.

D6_MultiStakeholder.evidence: Individual user acceptance model; no multi-stakeholder design.

D5_Rigor.evidence: Survey scales validated empirically but as acceptance instrument, not platform evaluation rubric.

frameworks.citation: Kampa, R. et al. (2024). TRAM Framework. [⚠️ Full citation to be verified — journal and DOI not confirmed]
frameworks.doi_note: ⚠️ CRITICAL: Full bibliographic data not yet verified. Need to confirm: author full names, journal name, volume, pages, DOI. Search via Scopus: 'TRAM' AND 'language learning' AND 'technology readiness'.

D1_TECH.evidence: Technology Readiness dimensions cover partial technical quality but not system architecture.

D2_PED.evidence: Adaptation of TAM to language learning context provides some pedagogical grounding but limited.

D5_Rigor.evidence: Framework description not yet fully reviewed — pending full paper acquisition.

frameworks.citation: Essafi, K. (2025). MLLA Framework. [⚠️ Full citation to be verified — 2025 paper, very recent, Scopus indexing unconfirmed]
frameworks.doi_note: ⚠️ CRITICAL: Very recent (2025). Full bibliographic data not confirmed. Verify: author name, journal, DOI, Scopus indexing status. Search: 'Essafi' AND 'MALL' AND 'evaluation' on Scopus.

D1_TECH.evidence: 3-aspect framework includes technical aspects but limited operationalization.

D2_PED.evidence: Linguistic and pedagogical aspects are the core of MLLA framework.

D4_HighStakes.evidence: More recent framework — addresses some test-aligned content. However, IELTS/TOEFL specificity not explicit.

D5_Rigor.evidence: Preliminary framework; pending full paper review.
